skill.name: swiftui-view-refactor
skill.description: Refactor and review SwiftUI view files for consistent structure, dependency injection, Observation usage, and MV-first state handling.
skill.summary: Guide AI assistants to clean up SwiftUI views while preserving behavior and SwiftUI data flow.
skill.author: Dimillian

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, network, reconnaissance, C2, and weak cryptography patterns. Review found false positives in Markdown, SwiftUI examples, SwiftData fetch examples, and ordinary prose. No evidence found of scripts, exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Spans Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted Markdown backticks around Swift property names and examples as Ruby or shell execution. These lines are documentation and SwiftUI examples, with no runnable shell, Ruby, install hook, or command execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside Markdown prose or fenced Swift examples. No executable skill script or command invocation is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SwiftData Fetch Flagged as Network Access
low_findings.description: The network finding points to a SwiftData modelContext.fetch example. It demonstrates local model fetching in Swift and does not call a network API or send data to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is a SwiftData FetchDescriptor and modelContext.fetch example. There is no URL, HTTP client, or data exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Blocker Keywords and Weak Crypto Matches in Prose
low_findings.description: The high-severity blocker hits match ordinary words, WWDC references, or SwiftUI article text rather than C2 behavior or cryptographic algorithms. No evidence found of command-and-control logic, encryption misuse, payload staging, or obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are SwiftUI architecture guidance and code examples. The suspicious labels do not correspond to actual cryptographic functions or C2 endpoints.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Refactoring Guidance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings point to guidance about avoiding bootstrap patterns, manual data fetching, and passing observable state. This is SwiftUI architecture advice, not host discovery or environment probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are natural-language refactoring guidance. They do not instruct the assistant to inspect host details, enumerate files, or collect system information.

content.value_statement: Large SwiftUI views can drift into inconsistent state handling and unclear dependency flow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to reorganize views around SwiftUI data flow, Observation, and MV-first patterns.
actual_capabilities: Orders SwiftUI view properties, initializers, body, helpers, and async functions consistently.
Applies MV-first guidance before introducing or expanding view models.
Reviews existing view model usage and prefers non-optional state when possible.
Guides dependency injection through initializers and SwiftUI environment values.
Suggests splitting large view bodies into smaller views or grouped extensions.
limitations: Does not provide an automated parser or formatter for Swift source files.
Does not replace project-specific architecture decisions or team conventions.
Does not validate runtime behavior without tests or app context.
Focuses on SwiftUI views, not UIKit controllers or backend Swift code.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a Growing Feature View
use_cases.description: Reorder state, dependencies, body content, and helper functions without changing user-visible behavior.

use_cases.title: Review View Model Usage
use_cases.description: Decide whether an existing SwiftUI view should keep a view model or move orchestration closer to the view.

use_cases.title: Standardize Observation Patterns
use_cases.description: Align root observable storage, dependency passing, and child view inputs with modern SwiftUI guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review One View
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single SwiftUI file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SwiftUI view for ordering, state placement, dependency injection, and Observation usage. Suggest focused changes that preserve behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Without Behavior Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate cleanup for a feature file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this SwiftUI view using the swiftui-view-refactor guidance. Keep layout and business logic unchanged, and explain any structural changes.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate View Model Necessity
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this SwiftUI view and its view model. Decide what should remain in the view model, what belongs in services, and what can move to view state.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Large View Split
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refactor planning across a large file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a refactor plan for this large SwiftUI view. Group helpers with clear marks, identify subviews to extract, and preserve public behavior.

output_examples.input: A SwiftUI view mixes environment values, state, body sections, and async helpers in one long block.
output_examples.output: A review that recommends a clear property order, smaller body sections, and grouped helper functions while preserving layout.

output_examples.input: A view owns an optional view model that is initialized after the first render.
output_examples.output: A refactor recommendation that initializes a non-optional observable model through the view initializer when project context allows it.

output_examples.input: A feature team asks whether a new SwiftUI screen needs a view model.
output_examples.output: An architecture note that compares MV-first state, environment services, and view model tradeoffs for the specific screen.

best_practices: Ask the assistant to preserve behavior unless a functional change is explicitly required.
Provide the full SwiftUI view and nearby model or service definitions when available.
Run project tests and previews after accepting structural refactors.
anti_patterns: Do not introduce a view model only to move simple SwiftUI state out of the view.
Do not ask for broad architecture rewrites without sharing project conventions.
Do not accept refactors that change navigation, persistence, or async behavior without review.

faq.question: Does this skill edit SwiftUI files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance that an AI assistant can apply when reviewing or refactoring provided SwiftUI code.

faq.question: Can it work with existing view models?
faq.answer: Yes. It reviews existing view models and prefers safer initialization and dependency passing when a view model is needed.

faq.question: Does it require SwiftData?
faq.answer: No. SwiftData appears only as architectural guidance for views that already use SwiftData patterns.

faq.question: Will it change my app architecture?
faq.answer: It should not change architecture by default. Use it to make scoped refactors that match your project constraints.

faq.question: Is this useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps reviewers check ordering, Observation usage, dependency flow, and unnecessary view model complexity.

faq.question: Which assistants can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
